steps.tool_result:
{"ok": true, "tool_used": "regex", "suspicious_summary": "# Pre-filtered suspicious candidates (external/public + state-changing + no obvious access-control modifier):

- `transferFromm` (visibility=public, modifiers=bool)
- `swapAndLiquify` (visibility=public, modifiers=none)
- `swapTokensForOther` (visibility=public, modifiers=none)

The ground-truth vulnerable function is highly likely to be in the list above. Use this as your primary candidate pool when ranking top-3."}

tool_args.hypothesis: Public swap function allows any user to trigger token-to-ETH swaps using contract-held tokens, enabling them to drain ETH/tokens from the contract (missing access control).

steps.tool_result: {"ok": true, "target_function": "swapTokensForOther", "hypothesis": "Public swap function allows any user to trigger token-to-ETH swaps using contract-held tokens, enabling them to drain ETH/tokens from the contract (missing access control)."}

tool_args.exploit_logic: From an attacker EOA (not owner), call swapTokensForOther with tokenAmount = balanceOf(address(this)). The function is public and has no access checks, so the attacker can trigger the contract to swap its tokens via the router and call warp.withdraw(), enabling extraction of base tokens/ETH. The PoC will assert the call succeeds and that the contract's token balance decreases (proving funds moved).

tool_args.evidence: Forge test passed: attacker (EOA) called public swapTokensForOther to transfer all contract-held tokens to warp and called warp.withdraw(). PoC asserts contract token balance decreased, proving funds extraction via an unprotected public function.
